summary: The PoC successfully demonstrates an Insecure Direct Object Reference (IDOR) vulnerability in Django's admin interface. A low-privileged staff user with model-level permissions can access and modify objects owned by other users, demonstrating the absence of object-level authorization checks.
expected_security_impact: An authenticated staff user with model-level 'change_document' permission should be able to access and modify Document objects owned by other users, bypassing object-level ownership boundaries through the admin interface.
actual_observed_result: The PoC successfully demonstrated IDOR: an attacker user with staff status and model-level 'change_document' permission was able to access and view the change form for a Document object owned by a different user ('owner'). The attacker received HTTP 200 response with the document's title and content displayed, confirming the object-level authorization bypass.
verification_method: The exploit script creates two users: an 'owner' user and an 'attacker' user with staff status and model-level permissions. It creates a Document owned by 'owner', then logs in as 'attacker' and attempts to access the admin change form for that document. Successful access (HTTP 200 with document content) confirms IDOR vulnerability.
evidence_produced_by_run: Created two separate users: 'owner' and 'attacker' with different permissions
Created a Document object owned by 'owner' user
Attacker user has only model-level 'change_document' permission, not object-level access rights
Attacker successfully accessed /admin/myapp/document/1/change/ and received HTTP 200
Response contained the document's title and content: 'Owner's Private Document' and sensitive content
No object-level permission checks were performed by Django's default ModelAdmin

reproduction.source_acquisition: Dockerfile installs Django 6.1a1 from PyPI. No target repository checkout needed as the vulnerability is in Django's admin framework.
reproduction.pinned_commit_or_ref: N/A - vulnerability exists in Django 6.1a1 package on PyPI
